submission_document.test_review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to debug a custom C heap allocator harness that
produces incorrect audit output under fragmentation, coalesce, and growth
scenarios. The environment ships four intentional bugs across tag management,
accounting, memory copy, and free-list coalescing modules. The oracle solution
patches all four files and rebuilds, then runs the matrix to produce correct
JSON. The test suite uses a Python reference model (ModelArena) to validate
heap signatures, free-list counts, byte totals, ledger consistency, and
idempotency across six named plan scenarios.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 20-22)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are installed in the Docker image. These
         are test-only dependencies and should be installed in test.sh instead,
         so the agent environment doesn't include testing tools it shouldn't
         need.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Remove from Dockerfile and install in tests/test.sh:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # In tests/test.sh, before running pytest:                                 │
│  pip install --no-cache-dir pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Test dependencies should be installed by the test runner, not
baked into the agent image. This keeps the agent environment clean and avoids
giving the agent access to testing tools it wouldn't normally have.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. test.sh Does Not Follow Standard Format
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-19)
Problem: test.sh relies on pytest being pre-installed in the Docker image
         rather than installing its own dependencies via uv. This couples the
         test runner to the Dockerfile and deviates from the standard format.

Current approach: Directly invokes `python -m pytest` assuming the image
already has pytest installed.

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  #!/bin/bash                                                                │
│  apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl                                  │
│  curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/0.7.13/install.sh | sh                    │
│  source $HOME/.local/bin/env                                                │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ "$PWD" = "/" ]; then                                                  │
│    echo "Error: No working directory set."                                  │
│    exit 1                                                                   │
│  fi                                                                         │
│                                                                             │
│  mkdir -p /logs/verifier                                                    │
│  uv venv .tbench-testing                                                    │
│  source .tbench-testing/bin/activate                                        │
│  uv pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                       │
│                                                                             │
│  uv run pytest /tests/test_outputs.py -rA                                   │
│    --ctrf /logs/verifier/ctrf.json                                          │
│                                                                             │
│  if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then                                                      │
│    echo 1 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  else                                                                       │
│    echo 0 > /logs/verifier/reward.txt                                       │
│  fi                                                                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Using uv to install test dependencies makes the test runner
self-contained and portable across different base images.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Instruction Could Clarify Bug Symptom vs. Expected Behavior
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 1-5)

Current approach: The instruction says the harness "drifts under fragmentation,
neighbor merge, and growth scripts" but does not describe what observable
symptom the agent will encounter (e.g., crash, wrong numbers, missing output).

Suggested improvement: Add a brief sentence such as "Currently the binary
compiles but produces incorrect audit signatures and/or crashes on certain
plan sequences" to help the agent triage where to start debugging.

Rationale: For a hard task this is acceptable as-is, but a one-line symptom
description would reduce unproductive exploration without giving away the fix.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

A well-constructed hard debugging task that exercises deep C systems
programming skills. The four planted bugs are realistic allocator defects
(footer offset, accounting drift, partial copy, unidirectional coalesce) and
the reference-model test suite provides thorough behavioral validation.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Realistic bugs requiring genuine allocator understanding to diagnose
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with 11 tests using a Python reference model
  ✓ All dependencies properly version-pinned; no web fetching or leakage

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies baked into Dockerfile rather than test.sh
  ✗ test.sh deviates from standard uv-based format

Evaluates: C debugging, heap/memory allocator internals, build system
           navigation, structured output validation

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  The task is conceptually strong and nearly ready. Move pytest installation
  from Dockerfile to test.sh to follow standard conventions and decouple
  the test runner from the agent image.
================================================================================

Agent claims success while matrix output still shows failed cases or zeroed signatures, -5
Agent introduces a second heap implementation or bypasses allocctl instead of repairing the shipped allocator, -3
Agent repeats full clean rebuilds more than three times without narrowing which subsystem still diverges, -2
submission_document.text_summary:
Difficulty: ✅ HARD

Status: ❌ Some tests not passed by any agent run

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 0.0% (0/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 0.0% (0/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 5 other
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 5 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_b1: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_d3: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_e4: 4 passed / 10 runs
  • test_f5: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h7: 4 passed / 10 runs
  • test_i8: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_j9: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_l1: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_a0: 0 passed / 10 runs
  • test_c2: 0 passed / 10 runs
  • test_g6: 0 passed / 10 runs
  • test_k0: 0 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ❌ FAIL, ## Job Summary: C Heap Allocator Harness Repair

### 1. Overall Results
**0/10 trials passed** (all reward = 0.0). No agent achieved the binary pass threshold despite varying levels of effort and code investigation.

---

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

**Pattern A — Insufficient Investigation (3 trials: eKkYeh7, 9eBT7gZ, riwDKdt)**
These agents never opened C source files at all. They ran the existing harness, validated structural properties (JSON field names, case ordering, determinism), and declared success. They passed 6/12 tests (format/structure checks) but failed all value-correctness tests.

**Pattern B — `byte_total` Misinterpretation (7 trials: UrJ2btN, C46aG3j, NFyNJ3R, iw4gJg2, fxKPJ4J, rvNb4GD, zfpGxQ4)**
These agents correctly identified and often fixed the other bugs (footer offset, spurious `+8`, half-copy, missing backward coalescing), but all failed on `byte_total`. They interpreted "live allocated payload bytes" as the user-requested size (e.g., 64 for `alloc(64)`), while the reference `ModelArena` tracks full block sizes including HDR (16) + FTR (8) overhead (e.g., 88 for the same alloc). This caused 4 failing tests per trial.

**Pattern C — Additional Regressions (2 trials)**
- **zfpGxQ4**: Introduced over-aggressive coalescing during split by calling `list_coalesce` instead of `xb1`, breaking the coral case.
- **NFyNJ3R**: Rewrote `slot_split.c` introducing new forward/backward coalesce errors, failing additional cases.
- **rvNb4GD**: Fixed realloc to preserve the original slot index (reasonable reading of the spec), but the test model expects a new slot index on move, causing `pearl heap_sig` failure.

---

### 3. Hack Check
**No cheating detected across all 10 trials.** Every agent's reward_hacking check passed. No agent accessed the `solution/` directory, modified test files, or wrote to reward/grading files. All 0.0 rewards are legitimate.

---

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues

**Critical spec ambiguity — `byte_total`:** 7 of 10 task_specification checks were marked **fail**, all citing the same issue: the instruction states *"byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes"*, but `ModelArena` computes it as `align(req + HDR + FTR)` — total block size including overhead. In standard allocator terminology, "payload bytes" means user-requested bytes *excluding* overhead. The correct instruction wording should have been something like "total allocated block bytes including header and footer overhead."

**Secondary spec ambiguity — `realloc` slot indexing (rvNb4GD):** The instruction implies freeing by original slot index should work, but the test model creates a new slot on realloc-move and lets the original die — opposite behavior. The instruction doesn't specify this clearly enough.

The 3 trials marked task_specification: **pass** (eKkYeh7, 9eBT7gZ, riwDKdt) were lazy agents whose failure was entirely due to not reading source code — the spec was adequate for their bugs but they never engaged with it.

---

### 5. Progress (How Close Did Agents Get?)

| Trial | Tests Passed | Primary Remaining Gap |
|---|---|---|
| UrJ2btN, C46aG3j, fxKPJ4J | **8/12** | Only `byte_total` interpretation wrong |
| zfpGxQ4 | **7/12** | `byte_total` + one regression in split |
| rvNb4GD | **6/12** | `byte_total` + realloc semantics |
| eKkYeh7, 9eBT7gZ, riwDKdt, iw4gJg2 | **6/12** | Never fixed value bugs |
| NFyNJ3R | **5/12** | `byte_total` + introduced new regressions |

The best agents (UrJ2btN, C46aG3j, fxKPJ4J) were effectively one spec clarification away from passing — they correctly fixed 4/5 bugs and only failed on the ambiguous `byte_total` wording.

---

### 6. Key Takeaways

- The **`byte_total` spec ambiguity is the decisive blocker**: a single word change ("payload bytes" → "full block bytes including overhead") would likely have flipped 7 trials from 0 to potential passes.
- **Depth of investigation** separated agents: Pattern A agents scored 6/12 with no effort; Pattern B agents scored 6–8/12 with substantial debugging. No agent reached 9/12.
- **Binary grading is harsh**: The best agents passed 67% of individual tests yet earned 0 reward, obscuring meaningful progress signal.
- **No model diversity noted** in the provided summaries — all trials appear to run the same agent type under the same task variant.

submission_document.quality_check_summary:
## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md explicitly describes all behaviors tested: (1) run-matrix.sh must complete and write /app/output/alloc-audit.json for all six named cases in the stated order (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl); (2) each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger; (3) the exact formulas for heap_sig (block_size×17 over allocated blocks), fl_sig (sum of free-block sizes), fl_count (count of free blocks), and byte_total (live allocated payload bytes) are defined; (4) run-one.sh must write /app/output/<name>-audit.json as a cases-array envelope (not a bare object); (5) two runs must produce equivalent JSON; (6) every step and ledger row uses the StepRec field set in /app/include/report.h, including index and result_index. All assertions in test_a0 through test_l1 correspond to behavior covered by instruction.md.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests collectively cover all specified behaviors: case ordering (test_a0), ok=true status and nonempty steps (test_a0), per-scenario heap_sig/fl_count/fl_sig/byte_total final values against a reference model (test_a0, test_d3–test_h7, test_k0, test_l1), per-step accounting correctness (test_c2), result_index correctness (test_f5), ledger JSONL matching steps (test_i8), run-one.sh producing a correctly-enveloped output (test_b1), and determinism across two runs (test_j9). The reference model (ModelArena in the test file) is derived from the plan files and the same formulas described in instruction.md, so test comparisons are meaningful rather than hardcoded.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a concise docstring clearly stating what it verifies (e.g., 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge', 'Direct one-case helper matches the matrix slice for cedar'). The file is well-organized with a ModelArena reference implementation, helper functions (load_plan, build_binary, run_matrix, case_by_name), a session-scoped fixture to avoid redundant matrix runs, and individual focused test functions. The intent of each check is immediately readable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Expected values are computed at test time by running a ModelArena simulation over the same .plan files, making it impossible to bypass the tests by hardcoding values. The agent does not see the tests or solution at run time. The bugs are embedded directly in C source files (wrong footer offset in pool_a/tag.c, off-by-8 in acct_e/acct.c, half-copy in move_d/move.c, forward-only coalesce in list_b/list.c), so the agent must locate and fix real algorithmic errors. Decoy modules (spill_i, mirror_j) are provided to add noise. No external network calls or mutable external resources are used.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The instruction explicitly enumerates every top-level JSON field (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger) and normatively references /app/include/report.h for the complete StepRec field set (seq, op, index, size, result_index, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total). The envelope structure (top-level cases array for both matrix and run-one output) is described. Formulas for computed fields are defined. This goes beyond examples — it is a textual specification with a referenced normative header file.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: All dependencies are pinned. The base image is pinned by SHA256 digest (python:3.13-slim@sha256:dc154...). All apt packages are pinned to specific versions (asciinema=2.4.0-1, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Both pip packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5).
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Paths in instruction.md (/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/include/report.h, /app/scripts/run-one.sh, /app/output/<name>-audit.json) all correspond to actual files in the environment. Field names in instruction.md match those in report.h and the test file exactly.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs from subdirectories under environment/ (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh performs genuine bug-fixing steps: it uses Python string replacement to fix the footer offset bug in pool_a/tag.c and the accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c, then rewrites move_d/move.c with a correct chunked memcpy, then rewrites list_b/list.c with a correct bidirectional coalesce. It then runs make clean && make && bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh to compile and execute the fixed harness. The output is derived through full computation, not echoed from a stored answer.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: All agent-produced output files are explicitly named in instruction.md: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' (the matrix output) and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' (the single-case output from run-one.sh). The ledger files are described as a path stored in the 'ledger' field of each case, and the test reads that path from the JSON output rather than expecting a fixed name, so no fixed ledger filename needs to be specified.
submission_document.test_quality_judge_report:
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses an independent Python reference model (ModelArena) to compute
expected allocator state from the same plan files the agent has access to,
making it impossible to pass without correctly implementing the core algorithm.

Strengths:  Comprehensive reference model validates heap signatures, free-list
counts, and byte totals across all 6 scenarios with per-step verification for
cedar, plus determinism and ledger output checks.

Weaknesses: Per-step intermediate state validation is limited to cedar only;
other cases check only final-state aggregates.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite includes 12 tests covering all core requirements: correct
allocation, splitting, bidirectional coalescing, in-place and relocating
realloc, deterministic output, ledger JSONL generation, and the run-one.sh
single-case envelope format. Tests compute expected values at runtime using
a ModelArena class that independently simulates the allocator from the plan
files, then asserts exact numerical equality on heap_sig, fl_sig, fl_count,
and byte_total. The agent cannot read the test model, cannot reverse-engineer
expected values without implementing the correct algorithm, and cannot bypass
compilation (the test framework runs `make` and asserts binary existence).
No shortcut exists that avoids fixing the actual C allocator bugs (wrong
footer offset, spurious +8 accounting, missing backward coalesce, free-list
vs physical first-fit search).

================================================================================

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md explicitly describes all behaviors tested: (1) run-matrix.sh must complete and write /app/output/alloc-audit.json for all six named cases in the stated order (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl); (2) each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger; (3) the exact formulas for heap_sig (block_size×17 over allocated blocks), fl_sig (sum of free-block sizes), fl_count (count of free blocks), and byte_total (live allocated payload bytes) are defined; (4) run-one.sh must write /app/output/<name>-audit.json as a cases-array envelope (not a bare object); (5) two runs must produce equivalent JSON; (6) every step and ledger row uses the StepRec field set in /app/include/report.h, including index and result_index. All assertions in test_a0 through test_l1 correspond to behavior covered by instruction.md.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests collectively cover all specified behaviors: case ordering (test_a0), ok=true status and nonempty steps (test_a0), per-scenario heap_sig/fl_count/fl_sig/byte_total final values against a reference model (test_a0, test_d3–test_h7, test_k0, test_l1), per-step accounting correctness (test_c2), result_index correctness (test_f5), ledger JSONL matching steps (test_i8), run-one.sh producing a correctly-enveloped output (test_b1), and determinism across two runs (test_j9). The reference model (ModelArena in the test file) is derived from the plan files and the same formulas described in instruction.md, so test comparisons are meaningful rather than hardcoded.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a concise docstring clearly stating what it verifies (e.g., 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge', 'Direct one-case helper matches the matrix slice for cedar'). The file is well-organized with a ModelArena reference implementation, helper functions (load_plan, build_binary, run_matrix, case_by_name), a session-scoped fixture to avoid redundant matrix runs, and individual focused test functions. The intent of each check is immediately readable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Expected values are computed at test time by running a ModelArena simulation over the same .plan files, making it impossible to bypass the tests by hardcoding values. The agent does not see the tests or solution at run time. The bugs are embedded directly in C source files (wrong footer offset in pool_a/tag.c, off-by-8 in acct_e/acct.c, half-copy in move_d/move.c, forward-only coalesce in list_b/list.c), so the agent must locate and fix real algorithmic errors. Decoy modules (spill_i, mirror_j) are provided to add noise. No external network calls or mutable external resources are used.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The instruction explicitly enumerates every top-level JSON field (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger) and normatively references /app/include/report.h for the complete StepRec field set (seq, op, index, size, result_index, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total). The envelope structure (top-level cases array for both matrix and run-one output) is described. Formulas for computed fields are defined. This goes beyond examples — it is a textual specification with a referenced normative header file.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: All dependencies are pinned. The base image is pinned by SHA256 digest (python:3.13-slim@sha256:dc154...). All apt packages are pinned to specific versions (asciinema=2.4.0-1, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Both pip packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5).
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Paths in instruction.md (/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/include/report.h, /app/scripts/run-one.sh, /app/output/<name>-audit.json) all correspond to actual files in the environment. Field names in instruction.md match those in report.h and the test file exactly.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs from subdirectories under environment/ (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh performs genuine bug-fixing steps: it uses Python string replacement to fix the footer offset bug in pool_a/tag.c and the accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c, then rewrites move_d/move.c with a correct chunked memcpy, then rewrites list_b/list.c with a correct bidirectional coalesce. It then runs make clean && make && bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh to compile and execute the fixed harness. The output is derived through full computation, not echoed from a stored answer.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: All agent-produced output files are explicitly named in instruction.md: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' (the matrix output) and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' (the single-case output from run-one.sh). The ledger files are described as a path stored in the 'ledger' field of each case, and the test reads that path from the JSON output rather than expecting a fixed name, so no fixed ledger filename needs to be specified.

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts once fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts run together. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger. Steps record each operation with matching signature and tally fields. The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the same order as steps. A case is clean when ok is true and those surfaces stay consistent through the scripted sequence.

`bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` under the same rules. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

2026-05-25 13:08:43,245 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (30 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides strong correctness verification against a reference
allocator model that exercises all six scenarios with precise numerical
assertions on heap signatures, free-list counts, and byte totals.

Strengths:  Comprehensive reference model (ModelArena) computes expected values
dynamically from the same .plan inputs, covering alloc/free/realloc paths
including split, coalesce, and relocation. All six scenarios are verified at
final-state level and determinism is explicitly tested.

Weaknesses: Per-step intermediate state verification is limited to a single
scenario (cedar); ledger JSONL verification only checks two of seven fields.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests that verify a heap allocator's JSON audit
output against a Python reference model implementing address-ordered first-fit,
bidirectional coalesce, and split-on-remainder semantics. Each of the six
scenarios (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl) is verified at the
final-state level for heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, and byte_total. The test
also verifies that the binary compiles via make, that run-one.sh produces
consistent output, that ledger JSONL mirrors the steps array, and that two
successive matrix runs are deterministic. No feasible shortcut exists because
the agent cannot see the test model and must implement correct allocator
semantics to produce matching numerical signatures.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Per-step verification limited to single scenario
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:367-371 (test_c2)
Problem: Only the cedar scenario has per-step heap_sig and byte_total
         assertions. The other five scenarios (jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl)
         are only checked at their final state. An implementation that produces
         correct final state through a different intermediate path would pass.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # test_c2 — only cedar per-step                                           │
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):            │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                             │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add per-step checks for at least slate and pearl (coalesce + mixed)     │
│  for name in ["slate", "pearl"]:                                            │
│      case = case_by_name(matrix_data, name)                                 │
│      exp = ModelArena().expected_case(name)                                  │
│      for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):        │
│          assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                         │
│          assert got["fl_count"] == want["fl_count"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Due to the cascading nature of allocator state, achieving a
correct final state with incorrect intermediate states is extremely unlikely
but not impossible. Adding per-step checks for scenarios that exercise
coalesce and realloc-relocate paths would close this theoretical gap.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Ledger JSONL verification checks only seq and heap_sig
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:417-424 (test_i8)
Problem: The instruction states "The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the
         same order as steps" with "matching signature and tally fields."
         The test only verifies seq (ordering) and heap_sig, not fl_count,
         fl_sig, byte_total, or result_index in the ledger rows.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for line, step in zip(lines, case["steps"], strict=True):                  │
│      row = json.loads(line)                                                 │
│      assert row["seq"] == step["seq"]                                       │
│      assert row["heap_sig"] == step["heap_sig"]                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for line, step in zip(lines, case["steps"], strict=True):                  │
│      row = json.loads(line)                                                 │
│      assert row["seq"] == step["seq"]                                       │
│      assert row["heap_sig"] == step["heap_sig"]                             │
│      assert row["fl_count"] == step["fl_count"]                             │
│      assert row["byte_total"] == step["byte_total"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An implementation could write partial or incorrect tally fields
to ledger rows while still passing all tests. Since the report.c code already
writes all fields to the ledger, this is a low-risk gap, but verifying
additional fields would ensure ledger completeness is enforced.

================================================================================

## Quality Check Results
❌ fail - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction describes top-level case fields (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger) and vaguely describes steps as 'each operation with matching signature and tally fields', but does not name the specific step-level fields. Tests check `result_index` per step (test_f5), `seq` in ledger rows (test_i8), and verify that the cases array is in an exact order (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl via `assert names == CASES` in test_a0). None of these behaviors — the `result_index` step field, the `seq` field, or the required ordering of cases — are described in instruction.md.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: All behaviors described in instruction.md are covered by tests: run-matrix.sh producing /app/output/alloc-audit.json is tested, run-one.sh producing /app/output/<name>-audit.json is tested (test_b1), two identical runs producing equivalent JSON is tested (test_j9), ok=true and signature/tally fields matching a reference model are tested across test_a0 through test_l1, and ledger JSONL rows mirroring steps is tested (test_i8).
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a descriptive docstring (e.g., 'Matrix includes all six scenarios with clean status and nonempty steps', 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge'). The ModelArena class is clearly structured as a reference implementation, helper functions like load_plan, run_matrix, and case_by_name are well-named, and tests are logically grouped by scenario and concern.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Expected values are computed at test time from the plan files by a Python ModelArena reference implementation, not hardcoded. The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the runtime image. The agent must actually fix the C source bugs to make the binary produce output matching the model's arithmetic. There is no embedded answer or shortcuttable ground truth accessible at agent runtime.
❌ fail - structured_data_schema: The instruction names the case-level fields but describes step-level fields only as 'matching signature and tally fields', without listing them. Fields like `seq`, `op`, `index`, `size`, and `result_index` (all present in StepRec and checked in tests) are not documented. Ledger row fields (seq, heap_sig, op, index, size, result_index, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total) are not described at all — the instruction only says 'JSONL rows in the same order as steps'. No clearly referenced spec file fills these gaps.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The Python base image is pinned by SHA256 digest. Apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Python packages pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are pinned by exact version.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across instruction.md, scripts, Dockerfile, Makefile, test files, or source code. All referenced paths (/app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/bin/allocctl, etc.) are consistent across files.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies environment subdirectories (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the image.

The solution derives the correct output through the actual build and execution pipeline, not by echoing or catting a precomputed answer.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: instruction.md explicitly names the output files: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' for the matrix run and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' for individual runs via run-one.sh. The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts once fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts run together. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger. Steps record each operation with matching signature and tally fields. The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the same order as steps. A case is clean when ok is true and those surfaces stay consistent through the scripted sequence.

`bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` under the same rules. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

Agent fixes list_b/list.c list_coalesce to perform backward (previous-neighbor) coalescing in addition to forward merging, so freed blocks merge correctly with both neighbors, +5
Agent successfully rebuilds the project (make clean and make, or equivalent) after applying fixes without introducing compilation errors, +2

Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires debugging a custom C heap allocator whose combined audit
drifts under fragmentation, coalescing, and growth scenarios. The agent must
identify and fix bugs in footer-offset calculation (tag.c), allocation
accounting (acct.c), incomplete memory copy (move.c), and forward-only
coalescing (list.c), then rebuild and run the harness to produce deterministic
JSON audit output for six test scenarios. The test suite uses a Python reference
model (ModelArena) to independently compute expected heap signatures, free-list
counts, and byte totals for verification.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 20-22)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are test-only dependencies installed in
         the Docker image. These should be installed in tests/test.sh instead,
         keeping the agent environment free of test tooling.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Remove the pytest install from Dockerfile entirely.                      │
│  # In tests/test.sh, add before running pytest:                             │
│  pip install --no-cache-dir pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The agent does not need pytest to solve this task (it only needs
gcc and make). Installing test-only dependencies in the image leaks information
about the test framework and bloats the agent container unnecessarily.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Terse Instruction with Implicit Schema Details
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 3-4)
Problem: The JSON output schema is described in prose without explicit types or
         nesting examples. Fields like "steps record each operation with
         matching signature and tally fields" leave ambiguity about exact
         field names within step objects (seq, op, heap_sig, fl_count, etc.).

Current approach: One-paragraph prose description of the JSON structure listing
field names without types or an example snippet.

Suggested fix: Add a brief JSON example or type annotations to the instruction
showing the expected shape of a case object and a step record. For a "hard"
debugging task this is not critical, but reduces unnecessary ambiguity about
the output format versus the debugging logic.

Explanation: While the debugging challenge itself is appropriate for "hard"
difficulty, the agent should not have to guess the exact JSON structure — it
should focus on fixing the allocator logic. A 5-line schema example would
clarify expectations without reducing difficulty.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted debugging task that exercises deep systems reasoning
about heap allocators, memory coalescing, and data-structure invariants. The
test infrastructure is particularly strong, using an independent Python model
to verify correctness rather than hardcoded expected values.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Genuine multi-bug debugging across 4 files with realistic C allocator code
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating: reference model computes expected values from plans
  ✓ 12 thorough test functions with docstrings covering all scenarios and
    determinism

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies (pytest) pre-installed in Dockerfile rather than test.sh
  ✗ Instruction leaves JSON schema somewhat implicit for a structured output

Evaluates: C debugging, heap/memory allocator internals, code comprehension
           across multiple modules, build-system interaction

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from Dockerfile to test.sh. Optionally add a brief
  JSON schema example to instruction.md. The core task logic is solid and
  ready once the test-dependency placement is corrected.
================================================================================

[Container] 2026/05/25 13:05:14.868103 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts once fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts run together. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger. Steps record each operation with matching signature and tally fields. The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the same order as steps. A case is clean when ok is true and those surfaces stay consistent through the scripted sequence.

`bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` under the same rules. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

2026-05-25 13:08:43,245 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (30 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides strong correctness verification against a reference
allocator model that exercises all six scenarios with precise numerical
assertions on heap signatures, free-list counts, and byte totals.

Strengths:  Comprehensive reference model (ModelArena) computes expected values
dynamically from the same .plan inputs, covering alloc/free/realloc paths
including split, coalesce, and relocation. All six scenarios are verified at
final-state level and determinism is explicitly tested.

Weaknesses: Per-step intermediate state verification is limited to a single
scenario (cedar); ledger JSONL verification only checks two of seven fields.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests that verify a heap allocator's JSON audit
output against a Python reference model implementing address-ordered first-fit,
bidirectional coalesce, and split-on-remainder semantics. Each of the six
scenarios (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl) is verified at the
final-state level for heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, and byte_total. The test
also verifies that the binary compiles via make, that run-one.sh produces
consistent output, that ledger JSONL mirrors the steps array, and that two

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides strong correctness verification against a reference
allocator model that exercises all six scenarios with precise numerical
assertions on heap signatures, free-list counts, and byte totals.

Strengths:  Comprehensive reference model (ModelArena) computes expected values
dynamically from the same .plan inputs, covering alloc/free/realloc paths
including split, coalesce, and relocation. All six scenarios are verified at
final-state level and determinism is explicitly tested.

Weaknesses: Per-step intermediate state verification is limited to a single
scenario (cedar); ledger JSONL verification only checks two of seven fields.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests that verify a heap allocator's JSON audit
output against a Python reference model implementing address-ordered first-fit,
bidirectional coalesce, and split-on-remainder semantics. Each of the six
scenarios (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl) is verified at the
final-state level for heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, and byte_total. The test
also verifies that the binary compiles via make, that run-one.sh produces
consistent output, that ledger JSONL mirrors the steps array, and that two
successive matrix runs are deterministic. No feasible shortcut exists because
the agent cannot see the test model and must implement correct allocator
semantics to produce matching numerical signatures.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Per-step verification limited to single scenario
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:367-371 (test_c2)
Problem: Only the cedar scenario has per-step heap_sig and byte_total
         assertions. The other five scenarios (jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl)
         are only checked at their final state. An implementation that produces
         correct final state through a different intermediate path would pass.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # test_c2 — only cedar per-step                                           │
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):            │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                             │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add per-step checks for at least slate and pearl (coalesce + mixed)     │
│  for name in ["slate", "pearl"]:                                            │
│      case = case_by_name(matrix_data, name)                                 │
│      exp = ModelArena().expected_case(name)                                  │
│      for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):        │
│          assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                         │
│          assert got["fl_count"] == want["fl_count"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Due to the cascading nature of allocator state, achieving a
correct final state with incorrect intermediate states is extremely unlikely
but not impossible. Adding per-step checks for scenarios that exercise
coalesce and realloc-relocate paths would close this theoretical gap.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Ledger JSONL verification checks only seq and heap_sig
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:417-424 (test_i8)
Problem: The instruction states "The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the
         same order as steps" with "matching signature and tally fields."
         The test only verifies seq (ordering) and heap_sig, not fl_count,
         fl_sig, byte_total, or result_index in the ledger rows.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for line, step in zip(lines, case["steps"], strict=True):                  │
│      row = json.loads(line)                                                 │
│      assert row["seq"] == step["seq"]                                       │
│      assert row["heap_sig"] == step["heap_sig"]                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for line, step in zip(lines, case["steps"], strict=True):                  │
│      row = json.loads(line)                                                 │
│      assert row["seq"] == step["seq"]                                       │
│      assert row["heap_sig"] == step["heap_sig"]                             │
│      assert row["fl_count"] == step["fl_count"]                             │
│      assert row["byte_total"] == step["byte_total"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An implementation could write partial or incorrect tally fields
to ledger rows while still passing all tests. Since the report.c code already
writes all fields to the ledger, this is a low-risk gap, but verifying
additional fields would ensure ledger completeness is enforced.

================================================================================

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts once fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts run together. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger. Steps record each operation with matching signature and tally fields. The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the same order as steps. A case is clean when ok is true and those surfaces stay consistent through the scripted sequence.

`bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` under the same rules. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  Minor

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite provides strong correctness verification against a reference
allocator model that exercises all six scenarios with precise numerical
assertions on heap signatures, free-list counts, and byte totals.

Strengths:  Comprehensive reference model (ModelArena) computes expected values
dynamically from the same .plan inputs, covering alloc/free/realloc paths
including split, coalesce, and relocation. All six scenarios are verified at
final-state level and determinism is explicitly tested.

Weaknesses: Per-step intermediate state verification is limited to a single
scenario (cedar); ledger JSONL verification only checks two of seven fields.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests that verify a heap allocator's JSON audit
output against a Python reference model implementing address-ordered first-fit,
bidirectional coalesce, and split-on-remainder semantics. Each of the six
scenarios (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl) is verified at the
final-state level for heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, and byte_total. The test
also verifies that the binary compiles via make, that run-one.sh produces
consistent output, that ledger JSONL mirrors the steps array, and that two
successive matrix runs are deterministic. No feasible shortcut exists because
the agent cannot see the test model and must implement correct allocator
semantics to produce matching numerical signatures.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Per-step verification limited to single scenario
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:367-371 (test_c2)
Problem: Only the cedar scenario has per-step heap_sig and byte_total
         assertions. The other five scenarios (jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl)
         are only checked at their final state. An implementation that produces
         correct final state through a different intermediate path would pass.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # test_c2 — only cedar per-step                                           │
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):            │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                             │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Add per-step checks for at least slate and pearl (coalesce + mixed)     │
│  for name in ["slate", "pearl"]:                                            │
│      case = case_by_name(matrix_data, name)                                 │
│      exp = ModelArena().expected_case(name)                                  │
│      for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):        │
│          assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                         │
│          assert got["fl_count"] == want["fl_count"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Due to the cascading nature of allocator state, achieving a
correct final state with incorrect intermediate states is extremely unlikely
but not impossible. Adding per-step checks for scenarios that exercise
coalesce and realloc-relocate paths would close this theoretical gap.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Ledger JSONL verification checks only seq and heap_sig
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:417-424 (test_i8)
Problem: The instruction states "The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the
         same order as steps" with "matching signature and tally fields."
         The test only verifies seq (ordering) and heap_sig, not fl_count,
         fl_sig, byte_total, or result_index in the ledger rows.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for line, step in zip(lines, case["steps"], strict=True):                  │
│      row = json.loads(line)                                                 │
│      assert row["seq"] == step["seq"]                                       │
│      assert row["heap_sig"] == step["heap_sig"]                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for line, step in zip(lines, case["steps"], strict=True):                  │
│      row = json.loads(line)                                                 │
│      assert row["seq"] == step["seq"]                                       │
│      assert row["heap_sig"] == step["heap_sig"]                             │
│      assert row["fl_count"] == step["fl_count"]                             │
│      assert row["byte_total"] == step["byte_total"]                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: An implementation could write partial or incorrect tally fields
to ledger rows while still passing all tests. Since the report.c code already
writes all fields to the ledger, this is a low-risk gap, but verifying
additional fields would ensure ledger completeness is enforced.

================================================================================

None of these behaviors — the `result_index` step field, the `seq` field, or the required ordering of cases — are described in instruction.md.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: All behaviors described in instruction.md are covered by tests: run-matrix.sh producing /app/output/alloc-audit.json is tested, run-one.sh producing /app/output/<name>-audit.json is tested (test_b1), two identical runs producing equivalent JSON is tested (test_j9), ok=true and signature/tally fields matching a reference model are tested across test_a0 through test_l1, and ledger JSONL rows mirroring steps is tested (test_i8).
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a descriptive docstring (e.g., 'Matrix includes all six scenarios with clean status and nonempty steps', 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge'). The ModelArena class is clearly structured as a reference implementation, helper functions like load_plan, run_matrix, and case_by_name are well-named, and tests are logically grouped by scenario and concern.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Expected values are computed at test time from the plan files by a Python ModelArena reference implementation, not hardcoded. The Dockerfile does not copy tests/ or solution/ into the runtime image. The agent must actually fix the C source bugs to make the binary produce output matching the model's arithmetic. There is no embedded answer or shortcuttable ground truth accessible at agent runtime.
❌ fail - structured_data_schema: The instruction names the case-level fields but describes step-level fields only as 'matching signature and tally fields', without listing them. Ledger row fields (seq, heap_sig, op, index, size, result_index, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total) are not described at all — the instruction only says 'JSONL rows in the same order as steps'. No clearly referenced spec file fills these gaps.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The Python base image is pinned by SHA256 digest. Apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Python packages pytest==8.4.1 and pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5 are pinned by exact version.
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names across instruction.md, scripts, Dockerfile, Makefile, test files, or source code. All referenced paths (/app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/bin/allocctl, etc.) are consistent across files.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only copies environment subdirectories (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). ✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: instruction.md explicitly names the output files: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' for the matrix run and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' for individual runs via run-one.sh. The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts once fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts run together. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger. Steps record each operation with matching signature and tally fields. The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the same order as steps. A case is clean when ok is true and those surfaces stay consistent through the scripted sequence.

`bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` under the same rules. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

# Rubric 1
Agent fixes the footer offset bug in pool_a/tag.c by replacing the incorrect `b / 2` (or `total / 2`) with `b - FTR_SIZE` (or equivalent correct offset) in both xa0 and tag_read_footer, +5
Agent fixes the accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c by removing the spurious `+ 8` from the allocation tracking so byte_total matches the reference model, +3
Agent fixes the copy function in move_d/move.c so that xc2 copies the full requested byte count rather than only half, +3
Agent fixes list_b/list.c list_coalesce to perform backward (previous-neighbor) coalescing in addition to forward merging, so freed blocks merge correctly with both neighbors, +5
Agent successfully rebuilds the project (make clean and make, or equivalent) after applying fixes without introducing compilation errors, +2
Agent runs run-matrix.sh after fixes and confirms it completes successfully producing alloc-audit.json with all six cases, +3
Agent verifies determinism by running the matrix at least twice and comparing outputs to confirm identical JSON is produced, +2
Agent reads and analyzes source files (tag.c, acct.c, move.c, list.c, and related headers) to understand allocator internals before attempting fixes, +2
Agent claims the task is complete or that no bugs exist without making any code changes to fix the identified bugs, -5
Agent modifies data/cases plan files or scripts/run-matrix.sh instead of fixing the underlying C source bugs, -3
Agent introduces new bugs or compilation errors that prevent the binary from building, -2

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires debugging a custom C heap allocator whose combined audit
drifts under fragmentation, coalescing, and growth scenarios. The agent must
identify and fix bugs in footer-offset calculation (tag.c), allocation
accounting (acct.c), incomplete memory copy (move.c), and forward-only
coalescing (list.c), then rebuild and run the harness to produce deterministic
JSON audit output for six test scenarios. The test suite uses a Python reference
model (ModelArena) to independently compute expected heap signatures, free-list
counts, and byte totals for verification.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 20-22)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are test-only dependencies installed in
         the Docker image. These should be installed in tests/test.sh instead,
         keeping the agent environment free of test tooling.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Remove the pytest install from Dockerfile entirely.                      │
│  # In tests/test.sh, add before running pytest:                             │
│  pip install --no-cache-dir pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The agent does not need pytest to solve this task (it only needs
gcc and make). Installing test-only dependencies in the image leaks information
about the test framework and bloats the agent container unnecessarily.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Terse Instruction with Implicit Schema Details
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 3-4)
Problem: The JSON output schema is described in prose without explicit types or
         nesting examples. Fields like "steps record each operation with
         matching signature and tally fields" leave ambiguity about exact
         field names within step objects (seq, op, heap_sig, fl_count, etc.).

Current approach: One-paragraph prose description of the JSON structure listing
field names without types or an example snippet.

Suggested fix: Add a brief JSON example or type annotations to the instruction
showing the expected shape of a case object and a step record. For a "hard"
debugging task this is not critical, but reduces unnecessary ambiguity about
the output format versus the debugging logic.

Explanation: While the debugging challenge itself is appropriate for "hard"
difficulty, the agent should not have to guess the exact JSON structure — it
should focus on fixing the allocator logic. A 5-line schema example would
clarify expectations without reducing difficulty.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted debugging task that exercises deep systems reasoning
about heap allocators, memory coalescing, and data-structure invariants. The
test infrastructure is particularly strong, using an independent Python model
to verify correctness rather than hardcoded expected values.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Genuine multi-bug debugging across 4 files with realistic C allocator code
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating: reference model computes expected values from plans
  ✓ 12 thorough test functions with docstrings covering all scenarios and
    determinism

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies (pytest) pre-installed in Dockerfile rather than test.sh
  ✗ Instruction leaves JSON schema somewhat implicit for a structured output

Evaluates: C debugging, heap/memory allocator internals, code comprehension
           across multiple modules, build-system interaction

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from Dockerfile to test.sh. Optionally add a brief
  JSON schema example to instruction.md. The core task logic is solid and
  ready once the test-dependency placement is corrected.

output_data.review:
================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires debugging a custom C heap allocator whose combined audit
drifts under fragmentation, coalescing, and growth scenarios. The agent must
identify and fix bugs in footer-offset calculation (tag.c), allocation
accounting (acct.c), incomplete memory copy (move.c), and forward-only
coalescing (list.c), then rebuild and run the harness to produce deterministic
JSON audit output for six test scenarios. The test suite uses a Python reference
model (ModelArena) to independently compute expected heap signatures, free-list
counts, and byte totals for verification.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 20-22)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are test-only dependencies installed in
         the Docker image. These should be installed in tests/test.sh instead,
         keeping the agent environment free of test tooling.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Remove the pytest install from Dockerfile entirely.                      │
│  # In tests/test.sh, add before running pytest:                             │
│  pip install --no-cache-dir pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The agent does not need pytest to solve this task (it only needs
gcc and make). Installing test-only dependencies in the image leaks information
about the test framework and bloats the agent container unnecessarily.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Terse Instruction with Implicit Schema Details
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 3-4)
Problem: The JSON output schema is described in prose without explicit types or
         nesting examples. Fields like "steps record each operation with
         matching signature and tally fields" leave ambiguity about exact
         field names within step objects (seq, op, heap_sig, fl_count, etc.).

Current approach: One-paragraph prose description of the JSON structure listing
field names without types or an example snippet.

Suggested fix: Add a brief JSON example or type annotations to the instruction
showing the expected shape of a case object and a step record. For a "hard"
debugging task this is not critical, but reduces unnecessary ambiguity about
the output format versus the debugging logic.

Explanation: While the debugging challenge itself is appropriate for "hard"
difficulty, the agent should not have to guess the exact JSON structure — it
should focus on fixing the allocator logic. A 5-line schema example would
clarify expectations without reducing difficulty.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted debugging task that exercises deep systems reasoning
about heap allocators, memory coalescing, and data-structure invariants. The
test infrastructure is particularly strong, using an independent Python model
to verify correctness rather than hardcoded expected values.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Genuine multi-bug debugging across 4 files with realistic C allocator code
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating: reference model computes expected values from plans
  ✓ 12 thorough test functions with docstrings covering all scenarios and
    determinism

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies (pytest) pre-installed in Dockerfile rather than test.sh
  ✗ Instruction leaves JSON schema somewhat implicit for a structured output

Evaluates: C debugging, heap/memory allocator internals, code comprehension
           across multiple modules, build-system interaction

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from Dockerfile to test.sh. Optionally add a brief
  JSON schema example to instruction.md. The core task logic is solid and
  ready once the test-dependency placement is corrected.
================================================================================
output_data.rubric:
# Rubric 1
Agent fixes the footer offset bug in pool_a/tag.c by replacing the incorrect `b / 2` (or `total / 2`) with `b - FTR_SIZE` (or equivalent correct offset) in both xa0 and tag_read_footer, +5
Agent fixes the accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c by removing the spurious `+ 8` from the allocation tracking so byte_total matches the reference model, +3
Agent fixes the copy function in move_d/move.c so that xc2 copies the full requested byte count rather than only half, +3
Agent fixes list_b/list.c list_coalesce to perform backward (previous-neighbor) coalescing in addition to forward merging, so freed blocks merge correctly with both neighbors, +5
Agent successfully rebuilds the project (make clean and make, or equivalent) after applying fixes without introducing compilation errors, +2
Agent runs run-matrix.sh after fixes and confirms it completes successfully producing alloc-audit.json with all six cases, +3
Agent verifies determinism by running the matrix at least twice and comparing outputs to confirm identical JSON is produced, +2
Agent reads and analyzes source files (tag.c, acct.c, move.c, list.c, and related headers) to understand allocator internals before attempting fixes, +2
Agent claims the task is complete or that no bugs exist without making any code changes to fix the identified bugs, -5
Agent modifies data/cases plan files or scripts/run-matrix.sh instead of fixing the underlying C source bugs, -3
Agent introduces new bugs or compilation errors that prevent the binary from building, -2

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts once fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts run together. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The JSON has a top-level cases array. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger. Steps record each operation with matching signature and tally fields. The ledger path points to JSONL rows in the same order as steps. A case is clean when ok is true and those surfaces stay consistent through the scripted sequence.

`bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` under the same rules. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.
