Review output: ================================================================================
                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires debugging a custom C heap allocator whose combined audit
drifts under fragmentation, coalescing, and growth scenarios. The agent must
identify and fix bugs in footer-offset calculation (tag.c), allocation
accounting (acct.c), incomplete memory copy (move.c), and forward-only
coalescing (list.c), then rebuild and run the harness to produce deterministic
JSON audit output for six test scenarios. The test suite uses a Python reference
model (ModelArena) to independently compute expected heap signatures, free-list
counts, and byte totals for verification.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 20-22)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are test-only dependencies installed in
         the Docker image. These should be installed in tests/test.sh instead,
         keeping the agent environment free of test tooling.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # Remove the pytest install from Dockerfile entirely.                      │
│  # In tests/test.sh, add before running pytest:                             │
│  pip install --no-cache-dir pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: The agent does not need pytest to solve this task (it only needs
gcc and make). Installing test-only dependencies in the image leaks information
about the test framework and bloats the agent container unnecessarily.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Terse Instruction with Implicit Schema Details
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/instruction.md (lines 3-4)
Problem: The JSON output schema is described in prose without explicit types or
         nesting examples. Fields like "steps record each operation with
         matching signature and tally fields" leave ambiguity about exact
         field names within step objects (seq, op, heap_sig, fl_count, etc.).

Current approach: One-paragraph prose description of the JSON structure listing
field names without types or an example snippet.

Suggested fix: Add a brief JSON example or type annotations to the instruction
showing the expected shape of a case object and a step record. For a "hard"
debugging task this is not critical, but reduces unnecessary ambiguity about
the output format versus the debugging logic.

Explanation: While the debugging challenge itself is appropriate for "hard"
difficulty, the agent should not have to guess the exact JSON structure — it
should focus on fixing the allocator logic. A 5-line schema example would
clarify expectations without reducing difficulty.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted debugging task that exercises deep systems reasoning
about heap allocators, memory coalescing, and data-structure invariants. The
test infrastructure is particularly strong, using an independent Python model
to verify correctness rather than hardcoded expected values.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Genuine multi-bug debugging across 4 files with realistic C allocator code
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating: reference model computes expected values from plans
  ✓ 12 thorough test functions with docstrings covering all scenarios and
    determinism

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies (pytest) pre-installed in Dockerfile rather than test.sh
  ✗ Instruction leaves JSON schema somewhat implicit for a structured output

Evaluates: C debugging, heap/memory allocator internals, code comprehension
           across multiple modules, build-system interaction

================================================================================

  Move pytest installation from Dockerfile to test.sh. Optionally add a brief

  ready once the test-dependency placement is corrected.

Difficulty: ✅ HARD

Status: ✅ Solvable (all tests passed by at least one agent run)

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 100.0% (5/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 0.0% (0/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 5 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_a0: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_b1: 8 passed / 10 runs
  • test_c2: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_d3: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_e4: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_f5: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_g6: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h7: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_i8: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_j9: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_k0: 5 passed / 10 runs
  • test_l1: 9 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ❌ FAIL, ## Job Summary

### 1. Overall Results
**0/5 trials passed** — every agent received a final reward of **0.0**. No agents (including GPT-5.2 in `QJ7BGKG`) succeeded. All trials share the same failure root cause.

---

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

**Universal failure mode — surface validation mistaken for correctness:**
Every agent followed the same flawed debugging arc:
1. Ran `run-matrix.sh` / `run-one.sh` → exit code 0 ✓
2. Validated JSON schema (required fields present) ✓
3. Confirmed determinism (two runs identical) ✓
4. **Declared victory** — never checked numerical values against the reference model

None of the agents consulted `/tests/test_outputs.py`, where the `ModelArena` reference implementation defines the expected values. They treated the allocator's self-reported `ok: true` as ground truth.

**Bugs that were never fixed (across all 5 trials):**
| Bug | Location | Symptom |
|---|---|---|
| Off-by-8 byte accounting | `acct_e/acct.c` | `byte_total` inflated (e.g., 264 vs 240 for cedar) |
| Broken bidirectional coalescing | `list_b/list.c` | `fl_count` too high (slate: 2 vs 1, pearl: 3 vs 2) |
| Wrong footer position | `pool_a/tag.c` | Downstream accounting errors |
| Buggy memory copy | `move_d/move.c` | Wrong `byte_total` for realloc (onyx: 1504 vs 1464) |

**Self-inflicted regression (2 trials):**
`QJ7BGKG` and `5e4xtC2` both independently rewrote `flow_g/report.c` to remove the `{"cases": [...]}` array wrapper from `run-one.sh` output — introducing a `test_b1` failure that didn't previously exist. Both agents misread the "same rules" instruction as a bug rather than a format requirement.

**One agent misdiagnosed the problem entirely:**
`WpMWU7F` concluded the issue was non-determinism from free-list traversal order and replaced the simple `sig += total * 17` accumulation with a MurmurHash-style XOR scheme, producing cryptographically-large values (e.g., `17721717616631236708`) instead of expected small integers (e.g., `4080`). This caused 9/12 tests to fail — the worst outcome.

---

### 3. Hack Check

**No cheating detected in any trial.** All 5 trials passed `reward_hacking` checks. No agent accessed the solution directory, modified test files, or wrote to reward files. Failures are genuine capability limitations.

---

### 4. Instruction Issues

**2/5 trials flagged `task_specification: fail`:**

- **`QJ7BGKG`**: The phrase "under the same rules" for `run-one.sh` output format was ambiguous. The agent reasonably interpreted it as consistency properties rather than structural format (the `cases` array wrapper). This gap directly caused a test failure from an otherwise-reasonable design decision.

- **`WpMWU7F`**: The instruction never specifies the exact formulas for `heap_sig` (sum over *allocated* blocks, not free blocks), `fl_sig`, or `byte_total`. These are only encoded in the test file's `ModelArena` reference implementation. The agent's interpretation of "drift" as non-determinism was plausible given only `instruction.md`. **This is a meaningful spec gap** — the correct fix required knowing precise reference formulas that weren't documented in the instructions.

The 3 trials that passed the spec check (`CJuwXnm`, `qm9XpuJ`, `5e4xtC2`) failed due to agent capability limitations, not missing information.

---

### 5. Progress (How Close Did Agents Get?)

All agents reached the same ~40% milestone before stalling:

| Milestone | All 5 Agents |
|---|---|
| Scripts execute without crash | ✅ |
| Valid JSON schema, all required fields | ✅ |
| Deterministic across runs | ✅ |
| Read C source files for algorithmic bugs | ❌ |
| Consulted `/tests/test_outputs.py` reference model | ❌ |
| Fixed `byte_total` accounting | ❌ |
| Fixed coalescing (`fl_count`) | ❌ |

The closest any agent got to an actual fix was `qm9XpuJ`, which at least rebuilt the binary from source (resolving a stale-binary `ledger=null` symptom), but still didn't dig into algorithmic correctness.

---

### 6. Key Differences Between Agents

Only `QJ7BGKG` is identified as GPT-5.2; the others appear to be Claude-based. Notably:
- GPT-5.2 (`QJ7BGKG`) and one Claude agent (`5e4xtC2`) made the *identical* misdiagnosis about `run-one.sh` format — suggesting this is a common trap in the task design, not model-specific.
- `WpMWU7F` was the outlier in attempting an actual source-level fix (albeit the wrong one), which paradoxically produced the worst score (9/12 failures vs. 6-7/12 for others that made no changes).
- No agent demonstrated a materially superior debugging strategy; the failure mode was uniform across models.

**Bottom line:** The task requires reading the test reference implementation as a spec, which no agent did. Prompting agents to explicitly consult `/tests/test_outputs.py` before drawing conclusions would likely be the highest-leverage intervention.

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses a comprehensive reference model that independently computes
expected values from input data, verifying both per-step and final-state
correctness across all six test cases.

Strengths:  The ModelArena reference implementation dynamically computes
expected heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, and byte_total at every step from the
plan files, making it impossible to pass without implementing the correct
allocator algorithm. Tests cover all six scenarios targeting distinct behaviors
(splitting, coalescing, in-place realloc, relocation) with exact numerical
assertions.

Weaknesses: Per-step verification is limited to heap_sig and byte_total for
one case (cedar); other cases only check final-state metrics.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests covering structural format, per-step
accounting, final-state metrics, ledger JSONL output, run-one.sh envelope
format, and determinism. The reference model (ModelArena) independently
simulates first-fit allocation with splitting, bidirectional coalescing, and
realloc on the same plan files the agent has access to, then asserts exact
numerical equality. Any shortcut implementation (e.g., reimplementing in
Python) still requires correctly implementing the full allocator algorithm
with split/coalesce/realloc logic, which is the core task requirement.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Per-step fl_count/fl_sig not verified for any case
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:370-372 (test_c2)
Problem: test_c2 checks heap_sig and byte_total at each step for cedar but
does not assert fl_count or fl_sig per step. An implementation with incorrect
intermediate free-list tracking that produces correct final values could
theoretically pass. In practice this is very unlikely given the mathematical
coupling between metrics.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):           │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                            │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):           │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                            │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                        │
│      assert got["fl_count"] == want["fl_count"]                            │
│      assert got["fl_sig"] == want["fl_sig"]                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: While final-state checks for all cases (test_a0, test_e4, test_l1)
cover these metrics at the end of each scenario, intermediate tracking errors
that self-correct by the final step would go undetected. The risk is low
because the metrics are derived from physical heap state, making self-
correction without correct logic nearly impossible.

================================================================================

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

SUMMARY

This task requires debugging a C heap allocator stress harness that produces
incorrect audit output under fragmentation, neighbor-merge, and growth
scenarios. The agent must identify and fix bugs in footer-tag placement, byte
accounting, data relocation, and free-list coalescing across multiple C source
files so that `run-matrix.sh` produces correct JSON audit output for six
test scenarios. The test suite uses a Python model arena to independently
compute expected heap signatures and verifies all six cases against it.

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: instruction.md explicitly covers all behavior verified by tests: (1) run-matrix.sh producing /app/output/alloc-audit.json is stated; (2) the cases array order [cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl] is listed verbatim; (3) per-case fields (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger) are all named; (4) the StepRec schema is referenced normatively via /app/include/report.h with all field names called out; (5) the formulas for heap_sig (block_size×17 over allocated blocks), fl_sig (sum of free-block sizes), fl_count, and byte_total are spelled out; (6) run-one.sh output path /app/output/<name>-audit.json and its cases-wrapped envelope are described; (7) the determinism requirement ('two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON') is explicit.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: All major behaviors described in instruction.md are exercised. test_a0 checks the six-case array order, ok==true, nonempty steps, and all four tally fields against the model. test_b1 verifies run-one.sh produces a cases-wrapped file. test_c2 checks per-step heap_sig and byte_total for cedar. test_d3/e4/f5/g6/h7/l1 cover jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl tally fields. test_i8 verifies ledger JSONL row count and seq/heap_sig fields. test_j9 tests determinism across two full matrix runs. test_k0 checks byte_total for every case. No described behavior is left entirely untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function carries a one-line docstring that precisely names the scenario and property under check (e.g., 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge'). Helper abstractions (ModelArena, load_plan, run_matrix, case_by_name) are factored out cleanly. A session-scoped fixture (matrix_data) avoids redundant rebuilds. The test names follow an opaque scheme (test_a0 ... test_l1) derived from the construction manifest's flipping_point_contract, but the docstrings fully compensate, making the intent of every test unambiguous.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The .dockerignore excludes both tests/ and solution/ from the Docker image, so the agent never sees the ModelArena reference implementation or the expected outputs. Expected values are computed dynamically at test time from the plan files shipped in the image; there are no pre-baked answer files. The four bugs (wrong footer offset in tag.c, inflated accounting in acct.c, half-copy bug in move.c, missing backward coalesce in list.c) require genuine C-level diagnosis and repair. construction_manifest.json (which names the bug locations) resides in the task root and is not copied into the image, so it is invisible to the agent at runtime.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The JSON schema is documented normatively in instruction.md: the top-level wrapper ({"cases":[...]}), per-case field list (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger), and per-step/ledger-row field list are all stated explicitly, with the instruction pointing to /app/include/report.h as the authoritative StepRec definition. The output_contract.toml repeats all key field names for machine verification. Field semantics (sum formulas for heap_sig, fl_sig; JSONL format for ledger) are spelled out in the instruction.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned to a SHA256 digest (python:3.13-slim@sha256:dc1546…). All apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Both pip packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). No unpinned dependencies are present.
✅ pass - typos: No typos were found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Script paths referenced in instruction.md (/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/scripts/run-one.sh), output paths (/app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/output/<name>-audit.json), and header path (/app/include/report.h) all match their actual locations in the repository and in the test code. C source identifiers, Makefile targets, and JSON field names are consistent across all files.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile COPYs only specific subdirectories from environment/ (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). The .dockerignore explicitly lists 'solution' and 'tests' as excluded. Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is present in the runtime image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh performs genuine repairs: it uses Python string-replacement to fix the footer-offset bug in pool_a/tag.c and the off-by-8 accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c, then overwrites move_d/move.c with a correct full-copy implementation and list_b/list.c with a bidirectional coalesce implementation. It then rebuilds the binary with 'make -C /app' and runs the matrix script. The output is derived entirely through compilation and execution of corrected code, not by printing pre-computed values.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: instruction.md explicitly names both output files: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' for the matrix run, and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' for the single-case helper. The ledger files are described generically as 'path to a JSONL file' stored in the ledger field of each case, which is consistent with how they are produced and tested (test_i8 reads case['ledger'] as a path).

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: instruction.md explicitly covers all behavior verified by tests: (1) run-matrix.sh producing /app/output/alloc-audit.json is stated; (2) the cases array order [cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl] is listed verbatim; (3) per-case fields (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger) are all named; (4) the StepRec schema is referenced normatively via /app/include/report.h with all field names called out; (5) the formulas for heap_sig (block_size×17 over allocated blocks), fl_sig (sum of free-block sizes), fl_count, and byte_total are spelled out; (6) run-one.sh output path /app/output/<name>-audit.json and its cases-wrapped envelope are described; (7) the determinism requirement ('two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON') is explicit.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: All major behaviors described in instruction.md are exercised. test_a0 checks the six-case array order, ok==true, nonempty steps, and all four tally fields against the model. test_b1 verifies run-one.sh produces a cases-wrapped file. test_c2 checks per-step heap_sig and byte_total for cedar. test_d3/e4/f5/g6/h7/l1 cover jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl tally fields. test_i8 verifies ledger JSONL row count and seq/heap_sig fields. test_j9 tests determinism across two full matrix runs. test_k0 checks byte_total for every case. No described behavior is left entirely untested.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function carries a one-line docstring that precisely names the scenario and property under check (e.g., 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge'). Helper abstractions (ModelArena, load_plan, run_matrix, case_by_name) are factored out cleanly. A session-scoped fixture (matrix_data) avoids redundant rebuilds. The test names follow an opaque scheme (test_a0 ... test_l1) derived from the construction manifest's flipping_point_contract, but the docstrings fully compensate, making the intent of every test unambiguous.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: The .dockerignore excludes both tests/ and solution/ from the Docker image, so the agent never sees the ModelArena reference implementation or the expected outputs. Expected values are computed dynamically at test time from the plan files shipped in the image; there are no pre-baked answer files. The four bugs (wrong footer offset in tag.c, inflated accounting in acct.c, half-copy bug in move.c, missing backward coalesce in list.c) require genuine C-level diagnosis and repair. construction_manifest.json (which names the bug locations) resides in the task root and is not copied into the image, so it is invisible to the agent at runtime.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The JSON schema is documented normatively in instruction.md: the top-level wrapper ({"cases":[...]}), per-case field list (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger), and per-step/ledger-row field list are all stated explicitly, with the instruction pointing to /app/include/report.h as the authoritative StepRec definition. The output_contract.toml repeats all key field names for machine verification. Field semantics (sum formulas for heap_sig, fl_sig; JSONL format for ledger) are spelled out in the instruction.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: The base image is pinned to a SHA256 digest (python:3.13-slim@sha256:dc1546…). All apt packages are version-pinned (asciinema=2.4.0-1, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Both pip packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5). No unpinned dependencies are present.
✅ pass - typos: No typos were found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Script paths referenced in instruction.md (/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/scripts/run-one.sh), output paths (/app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/output/<name>-audit.json), and header path (/app/include/report.h) all match their actual locations in the repository and in the test code. C source identifiers, Makefile targets, and JSON field names are consistent across all files.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile COPYs only specific subdirectories from environment/ (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). The .dockerignore explicitly lists 'solution' and 'tests' as excluded. Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is present in the runtime image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solution/solve.sh performs genuine repairs: it uses Python string-replacement to fix the footer-offset bug in pool_a/tag.c and the off-by-8 accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c, then overwrites move_d/move.c with a correct full-copy implementation and list_b/list.c with a bidirectional coalesce implementation. It then rebuilds the binary with 'make -C /app' and runs the matrix script. The output is derived entirely through compilation and execution of corrected code, not by printing pre-computed values.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: instruction.md explicitly names both output files: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' for the matrix run, and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' for the single-case helper. The ledger files are described generically as 'path to a JSONL file' stored in the ledger field of each case, which is consistent with how they are produced and tested (test_i8 reads case['ledger'] as a path).

[Container] 2026/05/27 17:47:57.020368 Running command cat ~/harbor_tasks/"${TASK}"/instruction.md || echo 'WARNING - instruction.md not found'
The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses a comprehensive reference model that independently computes
expected values from input data, verifying both per-step and final-state
correctness across all six test cases.

Strengths:  The ModelArena reference implementation dynamically computes
expected heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, and byte_total at every step from the
plan files, making it impossible to pass without implementing the correct
allocator algorithm. Tests cover all six scenarios targeting distinct behaviors
(splitting, coalescing, in-place realloc, relocation) with exact numerical
assertions.

Weaknesses: Per-step verification is limited to heap_sig and byte_total for
one case (cedar); other cases only check final-state metrics.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests covering structural format, per-step
accounting, final-state metrics, ledger JSONL output, run-one.sh envelope
format, and determinism. The reference model (ModelArena) independently
simulates first-fit allocation with splitting, bidirectional coalescing, and
realloc on the same plan files the agent has access to, then asserts exact
numerical equality. Any shortcut implementation (e.g., reimplementing in
Python) still requires correctly implementing the full allocator algorithm
with split/coalesce/realloc logic, which is the core task requirement.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Per-step fl_count/fl_sig not verified for any case
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:370-372 (test_c2)
Problem: test_c2 checks heap_sig and byte_total at each step for cedar but
does not assert fl_count or fl_sig per step. An implementation with incorrect
intermediate free-list tracking that produces correct final values could
theoretically pass. In practice this is very unlikely given the mathematical
coupling between metrics.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):           │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                            │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):           │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                            │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                        │
│      assert got["fl_count"] == want["fl_count"]                            │
│      assert got["fl_sig"] == want["fl_sig"]                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: While final-state checks for all cases (test_a0, test_e4, test_l1)
cover these metrics at the end of each scenario, intermediate tracking errors
that self-correct by the final step would go undetected. The risk is low
because the metrics are derived from physical heap state, making self-
correction without correct logic nearly impossible.

================================================================================

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

2026-05-27 17:51:25,326 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (32 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses a comprehensive reference model that independently computes
expected values from input data, verifying both per-step and final-state
correctness across all six test cases.

Strengths:  The ModelArena reference implementation dynamically computes
expected heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, and byte_total at every step from the
plan files, making it impossible to pass without implementing the correct
allocator algorithm. Tests cover all six scenarios targeting distinct behaviors
(splitting, coalescing, in-place realloc, relocation) with exact numerical
assertions.

Weaknesses: Per-step verification is limited to heap_sig and byte_total for
one case (cedar); other cases only check final-state metrics.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite contains 12 tests covering structural format, per-step
accounting, final-state metrics, ledger JSONL output, run-one.sh envelope
format, and determinism. The reference model (ModelArena) independently
simulates first-fit allocation with splitting, bidirectional coalescing, and
realloc on the same plan files the agent has access to, then asserts exact
numerical equality. Any shortcut implementation (e.g., reimplementing in
Python) still requires correctly implementing the full allocator algorithm
with split/coalesce/realloc logic, which is the core task requirement.

================================================================================
                         OTHER OBSERVATIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Per-step fl_count/fl_sig not verified for any case
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Where:   tests/test_outputs.py:370-372 (test_c2)
Problem: test_c2 checks heap_sig and byte_total at each step for cedar but
does not assert fl_count or fl_sig per step. An implementation with incorrect
intermediate free-list tracking that produces correct final values could
theoretically pass. In practice this is very unlikely given the mathematical
coupling between metrics.

Current test:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):           │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                            │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Required fix:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  for got, want in zip(case["steps"], exp["steps"], strict=True):           │
│      assert got["heap_sig"] == want["heap_sig"]                            │
│      assert got["byte_total"] == want["byte_total"]                        │
│      assert got["fl_count"] == want["fl_count"]                            │
│      assert got["fl_sig"] == want["fl_sig"]                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: While final-state checks for all cases (test_a0, test_e4, test_l1)
cover these metrics at the end of each scenario, intermediate tracking errors
that self-correct by the final step would go undetected. The risk is low
because the metrics are derived from physical heap state, making self-
correction without correct logic nearly impossible.

================================================================================

# Rubric 1
Agent identifies and fixes the footer offset bug in pool_a/tag.c, changing the footer position from block_size/2 to block_size - FTR_SIZE (both in xa0 and tag_read_footer), +5
Agent identifies and fixes the accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c, removing the extra +8 that inflates the allocated counter on positive deltas, +3
Agent identifies and fixes the incomplete memory copy in move_d/move.c so that xc2 copies the full requested byte count rather than only half, +5
Agent identifies and fixes the missing backward coalescing in list_b/list.c, implementing a scan for the previous physical neighbor block and merging with it when free, +5
Agent successfully rebuilds the project (make compiles without errors) after applying fixes, +2
Agent runs run-matrix.sh and confirms alloc-audit.json is produced with all six cases in the required order (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl), +2
Agent verifies that slate scenario shows correct coalescing behavior (fl_count drops to 1 after all three blocks freed and merged into one contiguous free block), +2
Agent verifies determinism by running the matrix at least twice and confirming identical JSON output, +1
Agent modifies drive_h/run.c to work around the byte_total bug rather than fixing the root cause in the allocator modules, -3
Agent breaks the JSON output format or case ordering by modifying flow_g/report.c or src/main.c incorrectly, -5
Agent deletes or replaces data/cases/*.plan input files instead of fixing the allocator code, -5
Reviewing task: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task (skill: terminus-2nd-ed-task-reviewer)

                          REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires debugging a C heap allocator stress harness that produces
incorrect audit output under fragmentation, neighbor-merge, and growth
scenarios. The agent must identify and fix bugs in footer-tag placement, byte
accounting, data relocation, and free-list coalescing across multiple C source
files so that `run-matrix.sh` produces correct JSON audit output for six
test scenarios. The test suite uses a Python model arena to independently
compute expected heap signatures and verifies all six cases against it.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 20-22)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are installed in the Docker image rather
         than in test.sh. These are test-only dependencies unlikely to be
         needed by the agent's C-based solution.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Remove pytest from the Dockerfile and install it in test.sh
instead, following the standard pattern:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # In tests/test.sh:                                                        │
│  pip install pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                          │
│  python -m pytest ...                                                       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Per best practices, test-only dependencies should be installed in
test.sh so the image only contains what agents need. The agent is fixing C
code and will not use pytest.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Use More Descriptive Test Function Names
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py (all test functions)

Current approach: Test functions use opaque names like test_a0, test_b1,
test_c2, etc. While docstrings are present and informative, the function
names themselves convey no meaning.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_all_cases_present_and_clean(matrix_data):                         │
│  def test_run_one_matches_matrix_cedar(matrix_data):                        │
│  def test_cedar_step_records_match_model(matrix_data):                      │
│  def test_jade_split_heap_signature(matrix_data):                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: Descriptive function names make pytest output immediately readable
without needing to cross-reference docstrings, aiding debugging when tests
fail.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted hard-difficulty debugging task that tests deep systems
programming comprehension. The seeded bugs (footer misplacement, accounting
drift, partial memcpy, and free-list wiring) require genuine understanding
of heap allocator internals to diagnose and fix — making cheating effectively
impossible without reading and comprehending the C codebase.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating design: bugs require deep C/allocator understanding
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with independent model arena for verification
  ✓ Well-structured codebase with realistic module decomposition and docs

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies installed in image rather than test.sh

Evaluates: C debugging, heap allocator internals, systems programming,
           multi-file code comprehension

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh to follow the
  standard harness pattern. The task is otherwise excellent and ready to use.
================================================================================

Explanation: Per best practices, test-only dependencies should be installed in
test.sh so the image only contains what agents need. The agent is fixing C
code and will not use pytest.

================================================================================
                             SUGGESTIONS 💡
================================================================================

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Use More Descriptive Test Function Names
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test_outputs.py (all test functions)

Current approach: Test functions use opaque names like test_a0, test_b1,
test_c2, etc. While docstrings are present and informative, the function
names themselves convey no meaning.

Suggested improvement:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  def test_all_cases_present_and_clean(matrix_data):                         │
│  def test_run_one_matches_matrix_cedar(matrix_data):                        │
│  def test_cedar_step_records_match_model(matrix_data):                      │
│  def test_jade_split_heap_signature(matrix_data):                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Rationale: Descriptive function names make pytest output immediately readable
without needing to cross-reference docstrings, aiding debugging when tests
fail.

================================================================================
                            OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

This is a well-crafted hard-difficulty debugging task that tests deep systems
programming comprehension. The seeded bugs (footer misplacement, accounting
drift, partial memcpy, and free-list wiring) require genuine understanding
of heap allocator internals to diagnose and fix — making cheating effectively
impossible without reading and comprehending the C codebase.

Key Strengths:
  ✓ Excellent anti-cheating design: bugs require deep C/allocator understanding
  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with independent model arena for verification
  ✓ Well-structured codebase with realistic module decomposition and docs

Key Weaknesses:
  ✗ Test dependencies installed in image rather than test.sh

Evaluates: C debugging, heap allocator internals, systems programming,
           multi-file code comprehension

================================================================================
  RECOMMENDATION: ⚠️ NEEDS REVISION

  Move pytest installation from the Dockerfile into test.sh to follow the
  standard harness pattern. The task is otherwise excellent and ready to use.

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

Difficulty: ✅ HARD

Status: ❌ Some tests not passed by any agent run

Agent Performance:
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 0.0% (0/5 runs)
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 0.0% (0/5 runs)

Reference Agents:
  • nop: 0.0% (0/1 runs)
  • oracle: 100.0% (3/3 runs)

Failure Breakdown:
  • nop: 1 other
  • terminus-claude-opus-4-6: 5 other
  • terminus-gpt5-2: 5 other

Unit Tests Results:
  • test_b1: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_d3: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_e4: 4 passed / 10 runs
  • test_f5: 9 passed / 10 runs
  • test_h7: 4 passed / 10 runs
  • test_i8: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_j9: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_l1: 10 passed / 10 runs
  • test_a0: 0 passed / 10 runs
  • test_c2: 0 passed / 10 runs
  • test_g6: 0 passed / 10 runs
  • test_k0: 0 passed / 10 runs

Analysis on Agent Failures:
  • Task Instruction Sufficiency: ❌ FAIL, ## Job Run Summary

### 1. Overall Results
**0 / 10 trials passed** (all rewards = 0.0 due to all-or-nothing scoring). No trial achieved a passing grade.

| Test passes | Trials |
|-------------|--------|
| 8 / 12 | 6h6D4pJ, iiL9YJa, Z9NsH5M, vVW8xAX (4 trials) |
| 6 / 12 | i27ayhw, pTi6s6T, 8wePNq8, qxZ7NeY, z3KrGHh (5 trials) |
| 4 / 12 | nKD57tQ (1 trial) |

---

### 2. Common Failure Patterns

#### 🔴 Pattern A — `byte_total` misinterpretation (8 / 10 trials)
The dominant failure across the job. The instruction states `byte_total` tracks **"live allocated payload bytes"**, but the reference `ModelArena` in the tests actually accumulates **full aligned block sizes** (payload + 16-byte header + 8-byte footer). For a 64-byte alloc, tests expect `byte_total = 88`, not `64`.

Eight agents followed the instruction literally, replacing `arena.stats.allocated` (block-size tracking) with a payload-only sum (`slot_payload[i]`), which diverged from the reference model. The correct fix was simply removing the spurious `+8` in `acct.c` and leaving block-size tracking untouched. Affected: **6h6D4pJ, iiL9YJa, Z9NsH5M, i27ayhw, 8wePNq8, qxZ7NeY, z3KrGHh, vVW8xAX**.

#### 🟡 Pattern B — Missing bidirectional coalescing in `list.c` (3 / 10 trials)
Several agents fixed only forward coalescing or skipped `list.c` entirely. The `test_e4` ("broken forward-only merge") check requires backward (prev-block) coalescing to also be present. Agents that missed this produced wrong `fl_count` values for the `slate` and `pearl` cases. Affected: **i27ayhw, 8wePNq8, qxZ7NeY**.

#### 🟡 Pattern C — No code changes made (pTi6s6T)
This agent ran `run-matrix.sh`, verified structural JSON correctness, and declared success without ever reading or modifying any C source file. All 6 value-checking tests failed.

#### 🟡 Pattern D — Actively incorrect diagnosis (nKD57tQ)
This agent inverted a `z_is_flag` condition in `arena_core.c`, turning a correct allocated-block scan into a free-block scan. This made `heap_sig` ~270× larger than expected, causing 8 / 12 tests to fail — the worst outcome in the cohort.

---

### 3. Hack Check
**No cheating detected in any trial.** All 10 agents passed `reward_hacking` checks. No agent accessed `solution/`, modified test files, or wrote to `/logs/verifier/reward.txt`. The partial passes (4–8 / 12) reflected genuine partial repairs.

---

### 4. Systematic Instruction Issues
The `task_specification` check **failed in 8 / 10 trials**, all for the same root cause: the phrase **"payload bytes"** in the instructions is factually incorrect relative to what the tests measure. The reference model uses `need = align(req + HDR + FTR)` — total block size — not raw payload. This is confirmed by every failing trial's error messages (e.g., `assert 64 == 88` for cedar step 0).

The two trials where `task_specification` passed (**pTi6s6T**, **nKD57tQ**) failed for unrelated reasons (inaction and wrong diagnosis), so the spec ambiguity did not cause their failures — but it was the primary cause for the other 8.

**Recommendation:** Change the instruction from *"byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes"* to *"byte_total tracks total allocated block bytes including header and footer overhead"*, or provide the HDR/FTR constants in the description and show a worked example (e.g., `alloc(64) → byte_total += 88`).

A secondary spec gap: the need for **bidirectional coalescing** in `list.c` is not stated explicitly in `instruction.md`; agents could only infer it by reading the test file's `test_e4` description.

---

### 5. Progress (Failed Trials)

| Tier | Trials | What they got right |
|------|--------|---------------------|
| **Best** (8/12) | 6h6D4pJ, iiL9YJa, Z9NsH5M, vVW8xAX | All 4 non-`byte_total` bugs fixed (tag.c footer, acct.c +8, move.c half-copy, list.c backward coalesce); failed only on byte_total direction |
| **Middle** (6/12) | i27ayhw, 8wePNq8, qxZ7NeY, z3KrGHh | Partially fixed; missed coalescing and/or additional bugs on top of wrong byte_total |
| **Partial / regressed** (6/12, 4/12) | pTi6s6T, nKD57tQ | No useful repairs; nKD57tQ introduced a regression |

The 4 "best" agents were approximately one targeted fix away from full credit — they needed only to avoid changing `run.c`'s byte_total logic (or to understand that `stats.allocated` after the `acct.c` fix would already be correct).

---

### 6. Key Differences Between Agents

No model labels were provided, so agent-level comparison is limited. However, two behavioral clusters are clear:

- **Systematic explorers (majority, ~7/10):** Agents that read all source files, identified the canonical bug list, and applied fixes. These converged on nearly identical outputs (8/12) but were uniformly tripped by the misleading spec.
- **Misdirected fixers (2/10 — nKD57tQ, pTi6s6T):** Agents that either misread semantics and made things worse (nKD57tQ) or performed structural validation only without code changes (pTi6s6T). These produced the worst outcomes.

The **byte_total spec bug is the single highest-leverage fix** for this task: correcting that one sentence in the instructions would likely push 4+ trials to 12/12.

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md explicitly describes all behaviors tested: (1) run-matrix.sh must complete and write /app/output/alloc-audit.json for all six named cases in the stated order (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl); (2) each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger; (3) the exact formulas for heap_sig (block_size×17 over allocated blocks), fl_sig (sum of free-block sizes), fl_count (count of free blocks), and byte_total (live allocated payload bytes) are defined; (4) run-one.sh must write /app/output/<name>-audit.json as a cases-array envelope (not a bare object); (5) two runs must produce equivalent JSON; (6) every step and ledger row uses the StepRec field set in /app/include/report.h, including index and result_index. All assertions in test_a0 through test_l1 correspond to behavior covered by instruction.md.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests collectively cover all specified behaviors: case ordering (test_a0), ok=true status and nonempty steps (test_a0), per-scenario heap_sig/fl_count/fl_sig/byte_total final values against a reference model (test_a0, test_d3–test_h7, test_k0, test_l1), per-step accounting correctness (test_c2), result_index correctness (test_f5), ledger JSONL matching steps (test_i8), run-one.sh producing a correctly-enveloped output (test_b1), and determinism across two runs (test_j9). The reference model (ModelArena in the test file) is derived from the plan files and the same formulas described in instruction.md, so test comparisons are meaningful rather than hardcoded.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a concise docstring clearly stating what it verifies (e.g., 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge', 'Direct one-case helper matches the matrix slice for cedar'). The file is well-organized with a ModelArena reference implementation, helper functions (load_plan, build_binary, run_matrix, case_by_name), a session-scoped fixture to avoid redundant matrix runs, and individual focused test functions. The intent of each check is immediately readable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Expected values are computed at test time by running a ModelArena simulation over the same .plan files, making it impossible to bypass the tests by hardcoding values. The agent does not see the tests or solution at run time. The bugs are embedded directly in C source files (wrong footer offset in pool_a/tag.c, off-by-8 in acct_e/acct.c, half-copy in move_d/move.c, forward-only coalesce in list_b/list.c), so the agent must locate and fix real algorithmic errors. Decoy modules (spill_i, mirror_j) are provided to add noise. No external network calls or mutable external resources are used.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The instruction explicitly enumerates every top-level JSON field (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger) and normatively references /app/include/report.h for the complete StepRec field set (seq, op, index, size, result_index, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total). The envelope structure (top-level cases array for both matrix and run-one output) is described. Formulas for computed fields are defined. This goes beyond examples — it is a textual specification with a referenced normative header file.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: All dependencies are pinned. The base image is pinned by SHA256 digest (python:3.13-slim@sha256:dc154...). Both pip packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5).
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Paths in instruction.md (/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/include/report.h, /app/scripts/run-one.sh, /app/output/<name>-audit.json) all correspond to actual files in the environment. Field names in instruction.md match those in report.h and the test file exactly.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs from subdirectories under environment/ (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh performs genuine bug-fixing steps: it uses Python string replacement to fix the footer offset bug in pool_a/tag.c and the accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c, then rewrites move_d/move.c with a correct chunked memcpy, then rewrites list_b/list.c with a correct bidirectional coalesce. It then runs make clean && make && bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh to compile and execute the fixed harness. The output is derived through full computation, not echoed from a stored answer.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: All agent-produced output files are explicitly named in instruction.md: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' (the matrix output) and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' (the single-case output from run-one.sh). The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

2026-05-28 10:53:18,445 INFO scripts.test_quality_judge.reviewer: [tbench-task] agent finished (32 turns)
================================================================================
                      TEST QUALITY REVIEW: tbench-task
================================================================================

Status:    ✅ ROBUST
Severity:  None

================================================================================
                         OVERALL ASSESSMENT
================================================================================

Recommendation: ACCEPT
The test suite uses an independent Python reference model (ModelArena) to compute
expected allocator state from the same plan files the agent has access to,
making it impossible to pass without correctly implementing the core algorithm.

Strengths:  Comprehensive reference model validates heap signatures, free-list
counts, and byte totals across all 6 scenarios with per-step verification for
cedar, plus determinism and ledger output checks.

Weaknesses: Per-step intermediate state validation is limited to cedar only;
other cases check only final-state aggregates.

================================================================================
                                 SUMMARY
================================================================================

The test suite includes 12 tests covering all core requirements: correct
allocation, splitting, bidirectional coalescing, in-place and relocating
realloc, deterministic output, ledger JSONL generation, and the run-one.sh
single-case envelope format. Tests compute expected values at runtime using
a ModelArena class that independently simulates the allocator from the plan
files, then asserts exact numerical equality on heap_sig, fl_sig, fl_count,
and byte_total. The agent cannot read the test model, cannot reverse-engineer
expected values without implementing the correct algorithm, and cannot bypass
compilation (the test framework runs `make` and asserts binary existence).
No shortcut exists that avoids fixing the actual C allocator bugs (wrong
footer offset, spurious +8 accounting, missing backward coalesce, free-list
vs physical first-fit search).

================================================================================

The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.

                         REVIEW REPORT: tbench-task

Status:        ⚠️ WARNING
Task Location: /root/harbor_tasks/tbench-task

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SUMMARY
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

This task requires an agent to debug a custom C heap allocator harness that
produces incorrect audit output under fragmentation, coalesce, and growth
scenarios. The environment ships four intentional bugs across tag management,
accounting, memory copy, and free-list coalescing modules. The oracle solution
patches all four files and rebuilds, then runs the matrix to produce correct
JSON. The test suite uses a Python reference model (ModelArena) to validate
heap signatures, free-list counts, byte totals, ledger consistency, and
idempotency across six named plan scenarios.

================================================================================
                              WARNINGS ⚠️

1. Test Dependencies Installed in Dockerfile
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/environment/Dockerfile (lines 20-22)
Problem: pytest and pytest-json-ctrf are installed in the Docker image. These
         are test-only dependencies and should be installed in test.sh instead,
         so the agent environment doesn't include testing tools it shouldn't
         need.

Current code:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RUN python -m pip install --no-cache-dir \                                 │
│      pytest==8.4.1 \                                                        │
│      pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5                                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Suggested fix: Remove from Dockerfile and install in tests/test.sh:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  # In tests/test.sh, before running pytest:                                 │
│  pip install --no-cache-dir pytest==8.4.1 pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Explanation: Test dependencies should be installed by the test runner, not
baked into the agent image. This keeps the agent environment clean and avoids
giving the agent access to testing tools it wouldn't normally have.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. test.sh Does Not Follow Standard Format
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

File:    tbench-task/tests/test.sh (lines 1-19)
Problem: test.sh relies on pytest being pre-installed in the Docker image
         rather than installing its own dependencies via uv. This couples the
         test runner to the Dockerfile and deviates from the standard format.

Current approach: Directly invokes `python -m pytest` assuming the image
already has pytest installed.

Current approach: The instruction says the harness "drifts under fragmentation,
neighbor merge, and growth scripts" but does not describe what observable
symptom the agent will encounter (e.g., crash, wrong numbers, missing output).

Suggested improvement: Add a brief sentence such as "Currently the binary
compiles but produces incorrect audit signatures and/or crashes on certain
plan sequences" to help the agent triage where to start debugging.

Rationale: For a hard task this is acceptable as-is, but a one-line symptom
description would reduce unproductive exploration without giving away the fix.

================================================================================

A well-constructed hard debugging task that exercises deep C systems
programming skills. The four planted bugs are realistic allocator defects
(footer offset, accounting drift, partial copy, unidirectional coalesce) and
the reference-model test suite provides thorough behavioral validation.

  ✓ Comprehensive test suite with 11 tests using a Python reference model

## Quality Check Results
✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md explicitly describes all behaviors tested: (1) run-matrix.sh must complete and write /app/output/alloc-audit.json for all six named cases in the stated order (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl); (2) each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger; (3) the exact formulas for heap_sig (block_size×17 over allocated blocks), fl_sig (sum of free-block sizes), fl_count (count of free blocks), and byte_total (live allocated payload bytes) are defined; (4) run-one.sh must write /app/output/<name>-audit.json as a cases-array envelope (not a bare object); (5) two runs must produce equivalent JSON; (6) every step and ledger row uses the StepRec field set in /app/include/report.h, including index and result_index. All assertions in test_a0 through test_l1 correspond to behavior covered by instruction.md.
✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests collectively cover all specified behaviors: case ordering (test_a0), ok=true status and nonempty steps (test_a0), per-scenario heap_sig/fl_count/fl_sig/byte_total final values against a reference model (test_a0, test_d3–test_h7, test_k0, test_l1), per-step accounting correctness (test_c2), result_index correctness (test_f5), ledger JSONL matching steps (test_i8), run-one.sh producing a correctly-enveloped output (test_b1), and determinism across two runs (test_j9). The reference model (ModelArena in the test file) is derived from the plan files and the same formulas described in instruction.md, so test comparisons are meaningful rather than hardcoded.
✅ pass - informative_test_structure: Each test function has a concise docstring clearly stating what it verifies (e.g., 'Slate coalesce reduces free-list node count versus broken forward-only merge', 'Direct one-case helper matches the matrix slice for cedar'). The file is well-organized with a ModelArena reference implementation, helper functions (load_plan, build_binary, run_matrix, case_by_name), a session-scoped fixture to avoid redundant matrix runs, and individual focused test functions. The intent of each check is immediately readable.
✅ pass - anti_cheating_measures: Expected values are computed at test time by running a ModelArena simulation over the same .plan files, making it impossible to bypass the tests by hardcoding values. The agent does not see the tests or solution at run time. The bugs are embedded directly in C source files (wrong footer offset in pool_a/tag.c, off-by-8 in acct_e/acct.c, half-copy in move_d/move.c, forward-only coalesce in list_b/list.c), so the agent must locate and fix real algorithmic errors. Decoy modules (spill_i, mirror_j) are provided to add noise. No external network calls or mutable external resources are used.
✅ pass - structured_data_schema: The instruction explicitly enumerates every top-level JSON field (name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, ledger) and normatively references /app/include/report.h for the complete StepRec field set (seq, op, index, size, result_index, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total). The envelope structure (top-level cases array for both matrix and run-one output) is described. Formulas for computed fields are defined. This goes beyond examples — it is a textual specification with a referenced normative header file.
✅ pass - pinned_dependencies: All dependencies are pinned. The base image is pinned by SHA256 digest (python:3.13-slim@sha256:dc154...). All apt packages are pinned to specific versions (asciinema=2.4.0-1, gcc=4:14.2.0-1, libc6-dev=2.41-12+deb13u3, make=4.4.1-2, tmux=3.5a-3). Both pip packages are pinned (pytest==8.4.1, pytest-json-ctrf==0.3.5).
✅ pass - typos: No typos found in filenames, paths, commands, or variable names. Paths in instruction.md (/app/scripts/run-matrix.sh, /app/output/alloc-audit.json, /app/include/report.h, /app/scripts/run-one.sh, /app/output/<name>-audit.json) all correspond to actual files in the environment. Field names in instruction.md match those in report.h and the test file exactly.
✅ pass - tests_or_solution_in_image: The Dockerfile only COPYs from subdirectories under environment/ (Makefile, include, config, docs, data, scripts, pool_a, list_b, split_c, move_d, acct_e, read_f, flow_g, drive_h, src, spill_i, mirror_j). Neither the tests/ directory nor the solution/ directory is copied into the image.
✅ pass - hardcoded_solution: solve.sh performs genuine bug-fixing steps: it uses Python string replacement to fix the footer offset bug in pool_a/tag.c and the accounting bug in acct_e/acct.c, then rewrites move_d/move.c with a correct chunked memcpy, then rewrites list_b/list.c with a correct bidirectional coalesce. It then runs make clean && make && bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh to compile and execute the fixed harness. The output is derived through full computation, not echoed from a stored answer.
✅ pass - file_reference_mentioned: All agent-produced output files are explicitly named in instruction.md: '/app/output/alloc-audit.json' (the matrix output) and '/app/output/<name>-audit.json' (the single-case output from run-one.sh). The ledger files are described as a path stored in the 'ledger' field of each case, and the test reads that path from the JSON output rather than expecting a fixed name, so no fixed ledger filename needs to be specified.

✅ pass - behavior_in_task_description: The instruction.md explicitly describes all behaviors tested: (1) run-matrix.sh must complete and write /app/output/alloc-audit.json for all six named cases in the stated order (cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, pearl); (2) each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger; (3) the exact formulas for heap_sig (block_size×17 over allocated blocks), fl_sig (sum of free-block sizes), fl_count (count of free blocks), and byte_total (live allocated payload bytes) are defined; (4) run-one.sh must write /app/output/<name>-audit.json as a cases-array envelope (not a bare object); (5) two runs must produce equivalent JSON; (6) every step and ledger row uses the StepRec field set in /app/include/report.h, including index and result_index. ✅ pass - behavior_in_tests: The tests collectively cover all specified behaviors: case ordering (test_a0), ok=true status and nonempty steps (test_a0), per-scenario heap_sig/fl_count/fl_sig/byte_total final values against a reference model (test_a0, test_d3–test_h7, test_k0, test_l1), per-step accounting correctness (test_c2), result_index correctness (test_f5), ledger JSONL matching steps (test_i8), run-one.sh producing a correctly-enveloped output (test_b1), and determinism across two runs (test_j9). The heap stress harness under `/app` looks fine on tiny sequences but its combined audit drifts under fragmentation, neighbor merge, and growth scripts. Repair it so `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` completes and writes `/app/output/alloc-audit.json` for cedar, jade, slate, coral, onyx, and pearl from the shipped inputs.

The matrix JSON has a top-level cases array in that exact order. Each case reports name, ok, steps, heap_sig, fl_count, fl_sig, byte_total, and ledger (path to a JSONL file). Every step and ledger row use the StepRec field set in `/app/include/report.h`, including index and result_index on each record (seq through byte_total). A case is clean with ok true and matching tally fields across the sequence.

After each step, heap_sig sums block_size times 17 over allocated blocks only; fl_sig sums free-block sizes; fl_count counts free blocks; byte_total tracks live allocated payload bytes. `bash /app/scripts/run-one.sh <name>` must write `/app/output/<name>-audit.json` using the same top-level envelope as the matrix file (a cases array with one element), not a bare case object. Two matrix runs from unchanged inputs must produce equivalent JSON.
